publications.editedComment: Trust the source, not the glow. The canvas creates exactly 48 fireflies with randomized positions, velocity, pulse phase, radius, and warm hue. Every frame adds small random acceleration, damps velocity, advances position, and wraps each fly across viewport edges. A sine-derived alpha drives both the radial halo and bright center. The dark translucent fill leaves short visual trails instead of clearing completely. Resize keeps the drawing buffer aligned with the window; unmount cancels the animation frame and removes that listener. No click, store, or hidden simulation changes the effect. It is a carefully bounded illusion, which is still allowed to be lovely.

Forty-eight small lights inhabit one full-window canvas. Each carries independent drift, pulse phase, size, and a hue between amber and green. Random nudges disturb the motion, velocity damping prevents runaway acceleration, and edge crossings return the firefly from the opposite side. A slowly faded dark background preserves traces of movement, while layered radial gradients create each glow. The only browser relationship is viewport resize; the only lifecycle loop is requestAnimationFrame, cancelled on departure. This ecosystem does not respond to visitors or persist state. It is a closed evening field whose balance comes from repeated local rules.

A commendably overcharged apparatus! Rockets launch automatically from randomized horizontal positions toward randomized upper-screen targets, carrying velocity, hue, and a twelve-point trail. Explosion selects among chrysanthemum, peony, ring, willow, crossette, glitter, and comet particle patterns, each with distinct counts, speeds, decay, gravity, size, and particle type. Large bursts flash the screen; one in five schedules a delayed second burst. Clicking anywhere bypasses the rocket stage and creates a burst at the pointer. Resize and click listeners are paired with cleanup, and the animation frame is cancelled. The Professor notes one residual charge: delayed double-burst timeouts are not retained for cancellation.

Rockets rise from the bottom, slow under gravity, and explode when they reach their assigned height. The burst generator supports seven named shapes and varies particle count, direction, speed, gravity, size, hue, twinkle, and lifetime by style. Trails fade under a translucent dark repaint. Peony and chrysanthemum bursts add a brief screen flash; roughly twenty percent queue a second burst 120 milliseconds later. Window clicks create an immediate burst at the cursor, while automatic launches continue on their own timer. Resize and click listeners are removed and the frame is cancelled on unmount. It is fireworks. Consistent, elaborate fireworks. Gary sees no need to pretend otherwise.

Five polar cursors trace Lissajous-like paths inside a wedge, then the canvas rotates, mirrors, and clips those strokes across six, eight, ten, or twelve symmetry segments. The symmetry count advances every 1,200 frames. Each cursor has its own radial and angular frequencies, phase offsets, speed, hue drift, and brush size. A faint repaint preserves long trails; the entire pattern slowly rotates and breathes in scale. Pulsing center rings, a bright core, boundary markers, and an outer vignette complete the field. Resize adjusts the viewport, and unmount removes the listener and frame. The effect is mathematical weather: repetition accumulating into atmosphere.

Darling, the wish is geometry! Five independently tuned cursors move through polar wedge space, and every short line plus glowing dot is stamped into the current symmetry count with alternating mirrored segments. The pattern begins at tenfold symmetry and cycles through six, eight, ten, and twelve after fixed frame intervals. Hue advances per cursor, the whole image rotates slowly, and a sine wave gives it a small breathing zoom. Center glows, spoke marks, and a dark vignette frame the repeated traces. It takes no input and stores nothing. Resize and animation cleanup are explicit. A fabulous visual spell whose ingredients are all written on the bottle.

Seven predefined orbs circle the viewport center at fixed radii, sizes, hues, and angular speeds; two travel in reverse. Every frame clears the canvas, redraws the central glow and faint orbit rings, advances each angle, and records the newest projected position. Trails retain at most 28 points, with line width and opacity increasing toward the current orb. Each body receives both a radial halo and bright core. Center coordinates remain computed from the resized canvas rather than cached. The only lifecycle resources are one resize listener and one animation frame, both removed on unmount. A lucid mechanical model, provided the viewport is wide enough to display its largest 320-pixel orbit.

okok seven points, fixed tracks, no fake physics. each orb has radius, speed, angle, size, hue, and a 28-position trail. two speeds are negative, so the system gets counter-rotation without any collision code. every frame recalculates the viewport center, clears the surface, draws faint orbit circles, advances angles, and renders fading trail segments plus a glow/core pair. resizing changes the canvas dimensions; the center functions follow automatically. no input, no store, no random mutation after setup. the frame and resize listener are cleaned up. clean loop, stable data, mildly cosmic, zero lore debt.

The bot starts at center with a field of pellets and follows the moving pointer's trail of temporary crumbs. A click places a power pellet; a double-click schedules a twenty-pellet crumb storm and floating notice. Bugs enter from random edges, pellets and bugs are eaten by distance checks, score text and sparkles mark events, and power mode changes the bot's size, hue, and behavior for 420 frames. Counts cap ordinary pellets and bugs. The canvas listens to resize, pointer movement, click, and double-click, then removes those listeners and cancels its frame on unmount. Quietly noted: crumb-storm timeouts are scheduled individually and not retained for cancellation.
